1.synthesis: A man of God from Judah cries against the altar at Bethel, prophesying that a future king, Josiah, will defile it, and gives a sign: the altar is torn and its ashes poured out (vv.2–3). When Jeroboam stretches out his hand to seize the prophet, it withers, and only the man of God’s prayer restores it (vv.4–6). The prophet refuses the king’s hospitality, having been commanded to eat nothing there (vv.8–9). The integrative thread is the LORD’s authoritative word against false worship: at the very inauguration of Jeroboam’s idolatry, God sends a prophet whose sign-confirmed oracle and the king’s withered hand declare that this altar stands condemned, and Josiah’s name is fixed in prophecy three centuries before his birth.
1.historical_context: The naming of Josiah (v.2) some 300 years in advance is a remarkable predictive prophecy fulfilled when Josiah desecrated Bethel’s altar (2 Kings 23:15–16). The withering and restoring of Jeroboam’s hand (vv.4–6) demonstrated the LORD’s power over the king. The prohibition against eating or returning by the same way (v.9) symbolized total separation from the apostate place and its worship.
1.literary_structure: This dramatic oracle (vv.1–10) immediately follows Jeroboam’s establishment of the calf-worship, God’s word confronting the new idolatry at its altar. The sign of the rent altar and the withered hand (vv.3–5) authenticate the prophecy, and the man of God’s strict obedience to his commission sets up the tragic sequel of his disobedience in the next section.
1.theological_application: God’s word stands against all false worship, and his prophecies are sure, naming Josiah centuries ahead (v.2; Isaiah 46:10). The withered-then-restored hand shows both God’s judgment and his mercy in answer to intercession (v.6). The man of God’s refusal of the king’s table teaches the importance of strict obedience to God’s specific command and separation from compromised worship, even when courtesy or advantage beckons (Psalm 1:1). The man of God’s refusal of the king’s table (vv.8–9) teaches the importance of strict obedience to God’s specific command and of separation from compromised worship, even when courtesy or reward beckons (Psalm 1:1). The withered-then-restored hand (vv.4–6) displays both God’s judgment on the one who opposes his word and his mercy in answer to intercession, a reminder that the same hand that smites also heals the penitent who cries to him (Hosea 6:1).
1.christology: The prophecy that a coming son of David, Josiah, would purge the false altar (v.2) points beyond Josiah to the greater Son of David who cleanses worship definitively, casting out all that defiles his Father’s house (John 2:15–16). The sure fulfillment of so distant a word testifies to the God whose every promise in Christ is Yes (2 Corinthians 1:20), and whose prophetic word concerning his Son cannot fail (Luke 24:44).

11.pericope_label: The Old Prophet and the Man of God
11.synthesis: An old prophet of Bethel pursues the man of God and lures him back with a lie: “an angel spoke to me… ‘Bring him back… that he may eat bread’” (v.18). The man of God disobeys God’s clear command, eats, and on his way is killed by a lion, which stands by the body without devouring it or the donkey (v.24). The old prophet buries him, affirming his oracle against Bethel will surely come to pass (v.32); yet Jeroboam does not turn (vv.33–34). The integrative thread is the deadly seriousness of obeying God’s word over any other claimed revelation: even a true prophet, deceived into disregarding God’s plain command, suffers judgment — a sobering lesson that no later “word,” however spiritual its claim, may override what God has clearly said.
11.historical_context: The old prophet’s lie (v.18) — claiming angelic authorization to contradict God’s direct command — tests the man of God, who fails by believing it. The lion that kills but does not devour, standing beside the donkey (vv.24–28), marks the death as supernatural judgment, not mere accident. The old prophet’s request to be buried beside him (v.31) and his confirmation of the oracle (v.32) show even he recognized the word’s certainty, fulfilled in 2 Kings 23:17–18.
11.literary_structure: This sequel (vv.11–34) turns the man of God’s earlier strict obedience into tragic disobedience, the lion-judgment (v.24) underscoring that God holds his messengers accountable. The chapter ends by returning to Jeroboam’s unrepentance (vv.33–34), framing the whole as a warning the king ignored, sealing the doom of his house.
11.theological_application: God’s clearly revealed word must not be set aside for any later claim of fresh revelation, however authoritative it sounds (v.18; Galatians 1:8, “even if… an angel from heaven should preach… a different gospel”). The man of God’s death warns that God holds his servants to obedience and that good beginnings do not excuse later compromise (Ezekiel 18:24). The believer must test every spirit against God’s established word and not be moved from it (1 John 4:1).
11.christology: The peril of departing from God’s sure word points to Christ, the Word made flesh, who perfectly obeyed the Father’s command even unto death (Philippians 2:8) and never wavered before any deceiving voice (Matthew 4:10). Where the man of God fell to a false word and died, Christ held fast to the true word and, dying in obedience, was raised to life. The certainty of the oracle against Bethel (v.32) confirms that every word of God finds its sure fulfillment in the One who is the faithful and true witness (Revelation 19:11).
